title: Applying a limewash finish
subtitle: A traditional matte chalk finish for lime plaster and old brick
excerpt: How to brush a traditional limewash onto an interior wall: thin the lime putty with water, dampen the wall, and build up two or three thin coats. Limewash hardens by reacting with the air rather than drying like emulsion, so it lets old plaster and brick breathe. About half a day of work for an average wall, with drying time between coats.
sourceNotes:
Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s), traditional lime-wash practice.

National Trust Manual of Housekeeping, open technical guidance on lime finishes.

English Heritage guidance on lime-based mortars and plasters (publicly available).

content.text: Limewash is the old way of finishing an interior wall. You thin lime putty with water to the consistency of milk, brush it on in thin coats, and let it harden by reacting with the air rather than drying like a paint. The finish is soft, matte, and slightly uneven, the right look for lime plaster, old brick, or natural stone. Half a day of brush work, with a couple of hours between coats for drying.
Limewash is not for modern gypsum plaster: it does not bond well to it. Use it on traditional lime plasters and bare masonry, where its main job, letting the wall breathe, actually matters.
What you need
Lime putty.

From a specialist lime supplier or a builders' merchant. A 25 kg tub covers about 15 to 20 square metres at three coats. The putty must have been stored wet for at least one month; fresh putty is too reactive for brush work.
Clean water.
For thinning the putty.
Earth pigment (optional).
Ochre, umber, sienna, and red iron oxide are stable in lime. Modern synthetic colours often fade in lime; test any pigment in a small batch first. Colours always look lighter when dry.
Wide brush, 50 to 100 mm.
A traditional limewash brush is wide with stiff bristles, but a 75 mm decorating brush works fine.
Protective gloves and goggles.
For handling undiluted lime putty.
Method
Mix the limewash. Lime is caustic, so wear protective gloves and goggles while diluting the putty. For the first coat, mix one part
lime putty

to two parts water by volume; that gives a thin primer wash. For later coats use one part putty to one and a half parts water. Stir thoroughly. The mix should run like semi-skimmed milk. If you are pigmenting, dissolve the dry pigment in the water before adding the putty.

Dampen the wall. Sponge or mist the wall with clean water before each coat. A dry wall pulls the water out of the limewash before it has time to harden, leaving a powdery surface that brushes off. The wall should feel damp but not running wet.

Brush on the first coat. Work the wash on with broad, crossing strokes in all directions to push it into the texture of the plaster. The first coat is thin and patchy, that is expected. Let it dry for at least four hours in a warm room, longer if the room is cool or damp.

Brush on the second and third coats. Re-dampen the wall between coats. Use the slightly thicker mix. Three coats is the standard for a sound, fully covered finish; each coat adds depth and shifts the tone a touch.
What a good result looks like

Limewash dries to a matte, softly textured finish that catches light differently from different angles. Brush marks are part of the look, not a fault. The colour when dry is noticeably paler than when wet.
Common mistakes
Brushing on a dry wall.
The wash dries before it bonds and rubs off as powder. Dampen the wall before every coat.
Mix too thick.
Heavy coats peel away from the surface as they dry. Thin coats, built up, give a stronger and more even result.
Brushing over gypsum plaster.
Limewash does not stick reliably to gypsum. Use emulsion or a clay-based paint on modern gypsum walls.
